speaker: interviewer
text: Four, three. Silvia Martin.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were describing the theaters in Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. The one was Beatrix Theater. I was there for-- and there were on stage big stars. Mostly German stars, which could not, you know, play in Germany anymore. Matter of fact, most artists couldn't work in other countries anymore, so except if they were lucky enough to get to Switzerland at an early time. Holland was just about the only place. And Amsterdam, specifically Amsterdam. And that went on for a while. And the first-- I can't even say Nazi-- but German I ever saw, floating in one of the wonderful canals in Amsterdam-- very dead, I must say. That was the first German I saw. And what happened, if you know Amsterdam, it has a lot of canals. Like Venice, except no gondoliers. And it's a marvelous place for drowning those lovely Germans. And the Dutch underground would stalk the German soldiers who went at night into the nightclubs and got very drunk, or into the red light district. And then at a certain moment, they would shove them into the canals. And then the next morning, they were fished out. They had their furlough.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's when I saw my very first German. It was wonderful. Then they got orders, the Germans, that they were not allowed anymore to walk alone at night. Only in groups, because so many disappeared or were fished out of the canals, which was wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And headquarters in Amsterdam, each one of the SS, the black shirts, the secret police, the military, they all had their-- their separate officers and their separate buildings. It was always a competition going on. I remember that I was trying to learn who was who, you know, which uniform means what.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the man in the-- the canal in uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. They were all German soldiers. They were all German soldiers, of course. You wouldn't put a-- a civilian in because you didn't know whether he was German or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were always very proud of their uniforms. Everybody wore uniforms. Except it was important then to know who is important, namely which uniform means what? Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: SS. Well, the SS! You knew there was a secret police. You certainly didn't want to run into them. But you had to learn, because there were so many different uniforms with so many different signs here on the collar or the medals, whatever they wore. So by the time I know-- I knew who was who, it was too late to make a difference. It didn't make any difference anymore. Holland was a country-- one of the richest in Europe because of the colonies which it still had then. And the Germans took their time. They plundered much faster in other countries than in Holland. It took quite some time before they really made beasts out of themselves. They-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they were-- they didn't-- they didn't interfere for a while, let's say that. They didn't interfere with one's so-called normal life. I changed then to another-- as I said, the Hollandsche Schouwburg. And the restrictions, the first restrictions, they did very softly. Came about, I think, 17 months into the invasion before they really started to be heard and to be seen all over the place. Once the restrictions started-- and there were every week practically, or perhaps every day different ones-- it became very, very rough. Slowly but surely, first the Jews were not allowed to visit, let's say, coffee houses and restaurants, or movies. The Jewish artists were not allowed to work anywhere but in one theater. The painters were not allowed to exhibit. The sculptors were not allowed to exhibit. Then the Jews were not allowed to ride taxis anymore. Then they weren't allowed to ride trams anymore. We had somebody there who was running the theater and he somehow managed to get permission to turn this theater into a Jewish theater. It became the Jewish theater-- the Joodsche Schouwburg-- with the understanding that only Jewish artists were performing on stage. Only Jewish artists in the orchestra, only Jewish costume designer, choreographers, anything which had to do was in this, and for Jewish audiences only. Because we all had IDs, which you had to show. And because of this, we had of course, incredible, incredible, entertainment there. Because you couldn't have afforded to hire even half of these people under normal circumstances, what was now on the stage. We had the soloists in the orchestra, and we had the best choreographers, and we had the best costume and set designers. It was fantastic. And the audiences, it was unbelievable. They couldn't wait to get to our theater. There came a time when these tickets sold on the black market for a lot of money. And the Christians, the non-Jews, bought these tickets and borrowed a Jewish ID card to get into it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So any time you looked through the curtain, you saw faces. And the average were not Jews, going in there with a friend's Jewish ID card. So it was a totally reversed role. There was then a while when we got a Nazi sitting in our loge every night. There was a lot played in Dutch, so he didn't understand most of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was also that whatever you said on stage has been censored. It was sent first to them, and they had to go through every script, so every script to see what was said. For instance, if there was a line which said, 'blond, blue-eyed, and gorgeous,' they crossed it out, because a Jew was never to be allowed to be blond, blue-eyed, or gorgeous. Stupid things like that. Still, I mean, this theater thrived. Then came the paintings was in our theater. The sculptor was in our theater. Then it was that the Jews were not allowed in coffee houses anymore. So we turned one-- on the third floor, one of the anterooms rooms-- into a coffee house. Had a sign outside, 'For Jewish audiences only,' every day from, till. And they were waiting in line to get to us dressed in their Sunday best.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then it was forbidden for Jews to get married in the City Hall. Synagogues were already closed. So were could they get married? Only in the Joodsche Schouwburg. So they were also married in our theater. So whatever took place in this theater on cultural activity, from A to Z, took place in this one theater.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then from one night to the next, I came to rehearsal with seven other places-- with seven other people. This theater was turned into a deportation center for Jews. The night before, there was one Nazi backstage while I was on stage. And he looked at everything. He didn't touch anything, and he was very quiet. And tiptoe. And I know that he bowed. And he said to me as I walked up-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, please. I hope I didn't, you know, disturb you. And I was totally flabbergasted. And I said, No, not at all. And-- and you know, nobody knew why. Why was he here? What did he do? Everybody was talking about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we decided he probably thought there was some-- something going on. You know, we were making bombs in there, or-- or-- or illegal press or something like that. We didn't know. And then the next day, he was back. And he told us, we who had been there-- there were seven of us-- we had to stay. And this was a deportation center now for Jews to be brought on into deportation. And if you didn't like it, we could volunteer and be on the first transport tonight. And then they came, our audiences, by the hundreds, by the hundreds, by the hundreds. And we had each a duty to perform, which was impossible to perform because we didn't have blankets. We didn't have water. We didn't have milk. We had nothing. It was a theater. We were not prepared for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And everybody wanted to know what's going to happen. Where are they going to send us? And why? And can you help us? And can you get us out?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came with candlesticks, and they came with blankets, and they came with books. And they dragged, and they dragged half the household behind. And we couldn't do anything. And then at night, trucks came. And off they went.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do? What was your job there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My job was to keep them away from the stage and to separate the children from the parents. Try. Try. You may as well, you know, climb Mt. Everest. You know, impossible. The whole thing was impossible, because there were about over 1,000 people at the time the trucks took them away. Now, the people who picked them up was the Dutch-- NSP-- a small group of Dutch Nazis. They worked for the Germans, and the Germans paid them $5 a Jew for each Jew they picked up. It is estimated, historically, statistically, that about 70,000 people went through the Schouwburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- it started at the end of '42. I don't know exactly, because I was already gone. I would say till the middle of '43, something like that. And figure about 1,100 people a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you? When did you leave there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- we were all there for five days and five nights. Then the Jewish Committee was formed. They took over, and we were let go home. In other words, we had involuntary-- we had to stay involuntary. We were not under arrest, but we were there involuntary. We were left-- allowed to go home. And life went on, such as it was. And by that time, even through the-- you know, they printed a lot of fake IDs, of course. There was one specific paper which was very hard to get. And for a while, you really-- it was worth a king's ransom. And that was a stamp in your ID which said that you're temporarily relieved or excused from labor transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, it was really temporary. It could be a day. It could be a month or six months. But you have to fulfill certain jobs for that. Why else would you get this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, for a while, there was a-- a man who owned a factory, used to own a factory, who had in his dining room converted 24 sewing machines. And he was working for the Germans with his own machines, by the way, which they took away and then lent him so he could do for the German Reich for their uniforms, the German uniforms. As a-- as a seamstress. I can't sew. I can't sew for-- for anything. And even so, he showed me. I mean, it was a catastrophe. Nothing even resembled a necktie. But I had this thing, because it was for-- for the German whatever-- temporary exempt from labor transport, which made you feel a lot safer. There's no doubt about it. This lasted six weeks. And then the Nazi came and closed the atelier. I wasn't even fired, you know, they just closed it. I don't know why. Too many neckties, not enough neckties, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had lost it again. And then it became rougher and rougher. Then you definitely could feel there was a presence. You know, we didn't have enough food anymore. We didn't have enough coal anymore. We didn't have enough this, enough this.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were every day other things. You could be picked up either from the house or from the street, whatever. Jews disappeared by the hundreds and hundreds. And there came a time when a Jew was really harder to find than a needle in a haystack. I mean, they were either already hidden, had left, or left openly under false-- you know, with false IDs. I was-- there was something going on, which I of course, didn't know until later. The Germans and the Jewish Committee, which were big, big, big, big people, tried to negotiate a deal with each other. While the Germans started with their nonsense about you can't do this anymore, you can't do that anymore, there was first we had to turn in our radios. Then it was we had to give all our jewelry and all our money. Then it was we were no longer allowed to speak to non-Jewish people, not on the street, not in the house. So every day, it was something else. The Germans knew that there was still a lot of Jewish fortunes left. And they wanted to make a deal with this Jewish community to exchange those still hidden Jewish fortunes for a list of people, certain people, the intellect. Engineers, doctors, teachers, or even actors. I happen to be-- I happen to be on that list.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know. I didn't-- I only knew when somebody knocked on our door one day. And the guy held a list-- two guys, two NSP people-- held a list, and my name was on that list. What this list was for and why my name was on it I did not know then. I had no idea. I was arrested and brought into my own theater. And there, I met a man-- or he was looking for me-- from the Jewish Committee. He told me about the list. And he told me why this list was and that they were trying to negotiate the deal. They didn't know whether it would work or not. Of course, meanwhile, there's always somebody in the Schouwburg, in that theater, trying to get them out one by one.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he would help me to get out that particular night. And how he did this is he brought me to the third floor-- which was the office of our producer, which used to be very beautiful with, you know, pictures of us and so on-- to stay there all night long and then escape over the flat roofs of Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there he already had a job for me also in the orphanage across the street, Catholic orphanage. Also again, was this temporary exempt from labor transplant. Now, at that time, there was already a German soldier standing watch inside and outside the theater. And this German soldier-- what he was supposed to watch, I really don't know, or whom-- had his room in this little office, which used to be very elegant and beautiful. It was like a monk's cell now. You know, just an army bed and Hitler's picture, cross and a little table. And he was paid by the Jews to watch one or two Jews at night so that they could escape over the roofs. So this guy worked for the German and the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was there to guard me that nobody would bother me at night. So I slept the first night when I was under arrest in our producer's formerly, now the soldier's room, with Hitler's picture right above me. And then in the morning, I-- which was very rough, over the flat-- not so flat always-- roofs of Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was pretty, pretty awful. Because you didn't know where you would end up and who would see you. And-- but I made it. Somehow I made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I got home, because it was very logical that the last place they would be looking for me is at home. Because according to them, I was already gone. I had already disappeared on the-- on the list. I was already on transport, right? So then I started working in the orphanage, in the Catholic orphanage. Mother Superior, scrubbing floors. She did not like actors at all. And-- but I was kind of safe. And that lasted for several weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I came one day and the orphanage was empty. The children were gone, the nuns were gone. There was nobody there except Mother Superior. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I didn't get to see her. I had looked in the-- little-- in the children's lockers. There was nothing there. In the nuns' rooms, there was nothing there. And I came back the next morning. The whole orphanage had changed. There were clothes hanging outside, winter clothes, all colors, lots of them. And a whole bunch of children were there, different children. What had happened, there was one Nazi who had decided that the Schouwburg, this theater, was not good for children. Even though they were being sent on transport a day or two later with their parents, this was no place for children. So they sent them to the orphanage-- which was not at all prepared, for we had no-- no-- nothing there-- to be taken care of them for a day or two or three until they go with their parents on transport. And there one night, I had the night shift. They had bombed. Even though there was no bombing in Amsterdam then, our building was on fire, and I was with the children in the basement. And I met there three Dutch guys from the underground who had come to meet me. They knew all about me. I knew nothing about them. The purpose was to help them save some of the children. The idea was to get me back on the day shift. And then we had permission-- Mother Superior had gotten permission to go with the children for a walk, weather permitting, around blocks. Always the same four blocks, three times. And at that time, on one-- the last block, there was a little house set way back from the street, like a little cloister. And one of the guys would be waiting there. And I would walk at the end, always at the end, with four or five children. Never more. And hold back, hold back. And just as the children turned the last corner, move them into where one of the guys stood. And then these children would be sent all over the country to farmers or-- or whoever was safe and would take them in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now everybody in the Schouwberg had name tags, which never made any sense. What for? This is the only time that it made sense, because that's where the children had name tags. You could take off the name tags and one of the other guys in the theater, in the Schouwburg, could look for the parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we walked around 4 o'clock, and the transport never left until 11:00, 11:30 at night. So plenty of time to find the parents of these children and tell them your children will be safe. They will be sent to such and such a place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this worked wonderful for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know, perhaps six weeks. And we got so many children that we could go two, three times. And you see, there again the-- the crazy thing, nobody ever counted the children. Nobody ever wondered that there were much more for lunch than there were for-- for dinner when we came back. Nobody counted them. So it was very easy. Easy. Not so easy. Because first of all, when it came to who do you choose, how you can make-- how can you make that decision which child should be safe and which child should not be safe? This is a terrible thing to do. When it came to it, I disappeared in the kitchen. I didn't want to make this decision.

speaker: interviewee
text: The children made it. They were so eager to go. So the ones who were quick and fast, they ran right out. So the shy ones and the timid ones, they were the ones at the end who kept holding on to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it worked wonderful for about six weeks. And then one day, Gus, the guy in-- who should have been in the door, wasn't there anymore. And I was told that I would be notified if there was anything wrong, somebody got wise to us or something, but I was not. Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just knew that it was over. I knew would end one day, just didn't know when. And he wasn't there anymore. Who was there instead, which I didn't know, there was a flower man who ran into me, putting tulips in my hands. As it turned out, he told me not to go home anymore, that they're wise to us, and to get out of here. Out of here. Not to come back to the orphanage. Not to go home. And that the other guys were-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], which means, you know, they were also under-- disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the names of any of the underground--

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Gus, Cor, and Wim. You know, it could be Joe, Joe, Joe. They weren't-- I'm sure they weren't Nazis-- they had three ordinary Dutch names. You know. I can describe them. But you don't introduce yourself to you and say my name is such and such. I live there and there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about Mother-- the Mother Superior? The one that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, the Mother Superior was a very interesting woman. She was-- I had no idea. I could not tell you whether she knew about this operation [INAUDIBLE] or not. I had no idea. I don't know what she knew and what she didn't know. I remember that she said to me when I came-- she was very unfriendly. She never offered me a chair. She said that she knows that as a Christian it is her duty to take care of everyone who is persecuted. But you know, she doesn't believe in actors, stars. They only stars she believes are up in heaven. I remember that. And she suggested prayer for me. She said that, you know, this exempt from labor transport is not going to save me, just going to buy me time. I remember she suggested prayer. I know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after they-- after they told you not to--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't go home. I wanted to go. I didn't know where to go. I had no idea where to go. I mean, at first thought I'd drop in at some friends in another part of town. But this you didn't do anymore. You could give someone a heart attack, you know, unexpected suddenly somebody rings a doorbell. You didn't ring doorbells anymore on-- you know, at that time without being expected. I went to my girlfriend's-- bless her soul and her heart-- in the same street opposite me. A beautiful, wonderful non-Jewish girl who saved many Jews. She was waiting for me already.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Margit Heinrich. She was a child of the circus. She grew up in a circus. She was a incredible, incredible-- beautiful inside and outside. And she was a child. And she truly believed love governs the world and-- and-- and badness can't last because it has no rewards. And she was guided by love. And she thinks whatever it is, how crazy it is, it's-- it's not gonna-- it's not gonna last. It can't. Magnificent.

speaker: interviewer
text: She started with playing-- it was a game for her in the beginning. It was only a game. So what are they going to do to me? You know, she was German nationality, even though she was fluent in Dutch. German passport. Non-Jewish. What are they going to do to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: First she started with going out on Friday buying kosher chickens for old frightened Jews who didn't dare to go out anymore. And she lit the candles, and she did the shopping for them. Then she was always in houses where she wasn't supposed to be anymore, like ours. Wasn't allowed anymore. She came to the theater. But it was just a game. And after I was arrested for the first time, things became much more difficult. And she started building a little place in her-- she-- way up in the attic. She had a tiny little room, a hiding place for Jews, which was impossible because her room itself was so very tiny. But she had built a contraption in her closet. It was ingenious. There was a closed up attic behind it. You couldn't sit the war out up there. But for a while, there were quite a lot, when they were running away, they went-- ended up at, as I always said, Margit's room at the top. It took 64 stairs to get up to her. And that was what was the safety, so called. Because by the time somebody rang the bell and somebody opened the door, and you could hear the creaking stairs, you had time to get into the hiding place. Now at that day, when I came home, I didn't go to my place. I went to her up there. And first of all, there were already two other girls there, which I didn't-- I mean, I knew them, but I didn't know them. I didn't even know whether they were Jewish or whatever. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were looking for me. They came around 12 o'clock at a time when even big Nazis weren't allowed on the street anymore. You know, because she said, this must be a drunk who's, you know, ringing the doorbell. It wasn't a drunk. They were looking for me-- three Nazis. And they tried to get up there, and they were that close to the door. And that's when I knew I could never, never sit in a room and hide out. I'd go mad. I'd go absolutely mad. The feeling of total helplessness. You sit there and you just wait for them to break down the door and get you. It's-- it's incredible. I mean, even if you run, you can't go anywhere but run. I heard them step by step. I heard them cursing. One broke a leg, I hope. Fell down the stairs and with the flashlight and-- and-- and screaming, I have enough. Chasing Jews every night. I'm going home! Dutch. They were Dutch. And it took forever for them to-- you could-- you could hear them breathe outside the door. And you were sitting there like a-- in a shooting gallery. A duck in a shooting gallery.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, your heart stops. And I think there are these seconds when you make up your mind. Do you really want to sit out the war in a chair in an attic? Can you do this without going mad? I don't know what it takes. I knew I couldn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these two girls had made arrangements with a big organization to leave the next morning to be brought with one guy over the border to Belgium, and somebody else then from Belgium to Switzerland. And they told me exactly how, where, what. They had heard about it from friends of theirs who had left that way, and it was very safe and very good. Cost 2,000 guilders, which was a fortune then. And you were told to bring, of course, all your jewelry, because you needed something to barter with to change. And money you could possibly bring. Only a little suitcase.
